Note that to use COSMOS APIs like tlm() you must pass globals(). locals (python only)	The locals to call eval with. Defaults to None. Note that if you're using local variables in a method you must pass locals().  Ruby ExamplePython Example elapsed = wait_check_expression("tlm('INST HEALTH_STATUS COLLECTS') > 5 and tlm('INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1') > 25.0", 10)

searchDocs.title: wait_check_packet​
searchDocs.content: Pauses the script until a certain number of packets have been received. If a timeout occurs the script will stop.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax # Returns the amount of time elapsed waiting for the packets elapsed = wait_check_packet("<Target>", "<Packet>", <Num Packets>, <Timeout>, <Polling Rate (optional)>, quiet)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget	The target name Packet	The packet name Num Packets	The number of packets to receive Timeout	Timeout in seconds. Script will stop if the wait statement times out waiting specified number of packets. Polling Rate	How often the comparison is evaluated in seconds. Defaults to 0.25 if not specified. quiet	Named parameter indicating whether to log the result. Defaults to true.  Ruby ExamplePython Example elapsed = wait_check_packet('INST', 'HEALTH_STATUS', 5, 10) # Wait for 5 INST HEALTH_STATUS packets over 10s

searchDocs.title: Limits​
searchDocs.content: These methods deal with handling telemetry limits.

searchDocs.title: limits_enabled?, limits_enabled​
searchDocs.content: The limits_enabled? method returns true/false depending on whether limits are enabled for a telemetry item.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax limits_enabled?("<Target Name> <Packet Name> <Item Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target of the telemetry item. Packet Name	Name of the telemetry packet of the telemetry item. Item Name	Name of the telemetry item.  Ruby ExamplePython Example enabled = limits_enabled?("INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1") #=> true or false

searchDocs.title: enable_limits​
searchDocs.content: Enables limits monitoring for the specified telemetry item.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax enable_limits("<Target Name> <Packet Name> <Item Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target of the telemetry item. Packet Name	Name of the telemetry packet of the telemetry item. Item Name	Name of the telemetry item.  Ruby ExamplePython Example enable_limits("INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1")

searchDocs.title: disable_limits​
searchDocs.content: Disables limits monitoring for the specified telemetry item.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax disable_limits("<Target Name> <Packet Name> <Item Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target of the telemetry item. Packet Name	Name of the telemetry packet of the telemetry item. Item Name	Name of the telemetry item.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disable_limits("INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1")

searchDocs.title: enable_limits_group​
searchDocs.content: Enables limits monitoring on a set of telemetry items specified in a limits group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax enable_limits_group("<Limits Group Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionLimits Group Name	Name of the limits group.  Ruby ExamplePython Example enable_limits_group("SAFE_MODE")

searchDocs.title: disable_limits_group​
searchDocs.content: Disables limits monitoring on a set of telemetry items specified in a limits group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax disable_limits_group("<Limits Group Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionLimits Group Name	Name of the limits group.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disable_limits_group("SAFE_MODE")

searchDocs.title: get_limits_groups​
searchDocs.content: Returns the list of limits groups in the system.  Ruby ExamplePython Example limits_groups = get_limits_groups()

searchDocs.title: set_limits_set​
searchDocs.content: Sets the current limits set. The default limits set is DEFAULT.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_limits_set("<Limits Set Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionLimits Set Name	Name of the limits set.  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_limits_set("DEFAULT")

searchDocs.title: get_limits_set​
searchDocs.content: Returns the name of the current limits set. The default limits set is DEFAULT.  Ruby ExamplePython Example limits_set = get_limits_set()

searchDocs.title: get_limits_sets​
searchDocs.content: Returns the list of limits sets in the system.  Ruby ExamplePython Example limits_sets = get_limits_sets()

searchDocs.title: get_limits​
searchDocs.content: Returns hash / dict of all the limits settings for a telemetry point.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_limits(<Target Name>, <Packet Name>, <Item Name>)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target of the telemetry item Packet Name	Name of the telemetry packet of the telemetry item Item Name	Name of the telemetry item  Ruby ExamplePython Example result = get_limits('INST', 'HEALTH_STATUS', 'TEMP1') puts result #=> {"DEFAULT"=>[-80.0, -70.0, 60.0, 80.0, -20.0, 20.0], "TVAC"=>[-80.0, -30.0, 30.0, 80.0]} puts result.keys #=> ['DEFAULT', 'TVAC'] puts result['DEFAULT'] #=> [-80.0, -70.0, 60.0, 80.0, -20.0, 20.0]

searchDocs.title: set_limits​
searchDocs.content: The set_limits method sets limits settings for a telemetry point. Note: In most cases it would be better to update your config files or use different limits sets rather than changing limits settings in realtime.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_limits(<Target Name>, <Packet Name>, <Item Name>, <Red Low>, <Yellow Low>, <Yellow High>, <Red High>, <Green Low (optional)>, <Green High (optional)>, <Limits Set (optional)>, <Persistence (optional)>, <Enabled (optional)>)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target of the telemetry item. Packet Name	Name of the telemetry packet of the telemetry item. Item Name	Name of the telemetry item. Red Low	Red Low setting for this limits set. Any value below this value will be make the item red. Yellow Low	Yellow Low setting for this limits set. Any value below this value but greater than Red Low will be make the item yellow. Yellow High	Yellow High setting for this limits set. Any value above this value but less than Red High will be make the item yellow. Red High	Red High setting for this limits set. Any value above this value will be make the item red. Green Low	Optional. If given, any value greater than Green Low and less than Green_High will make the item blue indicating a good operational value. Green High	Optional. If given, any value greater than Green Low and less than Green_High will make the item blue indicating a good operational value. Limits Set	Optional. Set the limits for a specific limits set. If not given then it defaults to setting limits for the CUSTOM limits set. Persistence	Optional. Set the number of samples this item must be out of limits before changing limits state. Defaults to no change. Note: This affects all limits settings across limits sets. Enabled	Optional. Whether or not limits are enabled for this item. Defaults to true. Note: This affects all limits settings across limits sets.  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_limits('INST', 'HEALTH_STATUS', 'TEMP1', -10.0, 0.0, 50.0, 60.0, 30.0, 40.0, 'TVAC', 1, true)

searchDocs.title: get_out_of_limits​
searchDocs.content: Returns an array with the target_name, packet_name, item_name, and limits_state of all items that are out of their limits ranges.  Ruby ExamplePython Example out_of_limits_items = get_out_of_limits()

searchDocs.title: get_overall_limits_state​
searchDocs.content: Returns the overall limits state for the COSMOS system. Returns 'GREEN', 'YELLOW', or 'RED'.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_overall_limits_state(<Ignored Items> (optional))   Parameter	DescriptionIgnored Items	Array of arrays with items to ignore when determining the overall limits state. [['TARGET_NAME', 'PACKET_NAME', 'ITEM_NAME'], ...]  Ruby ExamplePython Example overall_limits_state = get_overall_limits_state() overall_limits_state = get_overall_limits_state([['INST', 'HEALTH_STATUS', 'TEMP1']])

searchDocs.title: get_limits_events​
searchDocs.content: Returns limits events based on an offset returned from the last time it was called.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_limits_event(<Offset>, count)   Parameter	DescriptionOffset	Offset returned by the previous call to get_limits_event. Default is nil for the initial call count	Named parameter specifying the maximum number of limits events to return. Default is 100  Ruby ExamplePython Example events = get_limits_event() puts events #[["1613077715557-0", # {"type"=>"LIMITS_CHANGE", # "target_name"=>"TGT", # "packet_name"=>"PKT", # "item_name"=>"ITEM", # "old_limits_state"=>"YELLOW_LOW", # "new_limits_state"=>"RED_LOW", # "time_nsec"=>"1", # "message"=>"message"}], # ["1613077715557-1", # {"type"=>"LIMITS_CHANGE", # "target_name"=>"TGT", # "packet_name"=>"PKT", # "item_name"=>"ITEM", # "old_limits_state"=>"RED_LOW", # "new_limits_state"=>"YELLOW_LOW", # "time_nsec"=>"2", # "message"=>"message"}]] # The last offset is the first item ([0]) in the last event ([-1]) events = get_limits_event(events[-1][0]) puts events #[["1613077715657-0", # {"type"=>"LIMITS_CHANGE", # ...

searchDocs.title: Plugins / Packages​
searchDocs.content: APIs for getting knowledge about plugins and packages.

searchDocs.title: plugin_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the installed plugins.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax plugin_list(default: false)   Parameter	Descriptiondefault	Whether to include the default COSMOS plugins (all the regular applications)  Ruby ExamplePython Example plugins = plugin_list() #=> ['openc3-cosmos-demo-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250116214358.gem__20250116214539'] plugins = plugin_list(default: true) #=> # ['openc3-cosmos-demo-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250116214358.gem__20250116214539', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-admin-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200004.gem__20250116211504', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-bucketexplorer-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200008.gem__20250116211525', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-cmdsender-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200012.gem__20250116211515', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-cmdtlmserver-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200015.gem__20250116211512', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-dataextractor-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200005.gem__20250116211521', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-dataviewer-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200009.gem__20250116211522', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-docs-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250117042104.gem__20250117042154', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-handbooks-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200014.gem__20250116211523', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-iframe-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200011.gem__20250116211503', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-limitsmonitor-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200017.gem__20250116211514', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-packetviewer-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200004.gem__20250116211518', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-scriptrunner-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200012.gem__20250116211517', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-tablemanager-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200018.gem__20250116211524', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-tlmgrapher-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200005.gem__20250116211520', # 'openc3-cosmos-tool-tlmviewer-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115200008.gem__20250116211519', # 'openc3-tool-base-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115195959.gem__20250116211459']

searchDocs.title: plugin_get​
searchDocs.content: Returns information about an installed plugin.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax plugin_get(<Plugin Name>)   Parameter	DescriptionPlugin Name	Full name of the plugin (typically taken from plugin_list())  Ruby ExamplePython Example plugin_get('openc3-cosmos-demo-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250116214358.gem__20250116214539') #=> # { "name"=>"openc3-cosmos-demo-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250116214358.gem__20250116214539", # "variables"=>{"inst_target_name"=>"INST", ...}, # "plugin_txt_lines"=>["# Note: This plugin includes 4 targets ..."], # "needs_dependencies"=>true, # "updated_at"=>1737063941094624764 }

searchDocs.title: package_list​
searchDocs.content: List all the packages installed in COSMOS.  Ruby ExamplePython Example package_list() #=> {"ruby"=>["openc3-cosmos-demo-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250116214358.gem", ..., "openc3-tool-base-6.0.3.pre.beta0.20250115195959.gem"], # "python"=>["numpy-2.1.1", "pip-24.0", "setuptools-65.5.0"]}

searchDocs.title: Targets​
searchDocs.content: APIs for getting knowledge about targets.

searchDocs.title: get_target_names​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of the targets in the system in an array.  Ruby ExamplePython Example targets = get_target_names() #=> ['INST', 'INST2', 'EXAMPLE', 'TEMPLATED']

searchDocs.title: get_target​
searchDocs.content: Returns a target hash containing all the information about the target.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_target("<Target Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target.  Ruby ExamplePython Example target = get_target("INST") puts target # {"name"=>"INST", # "folder_name"=>"INST", # "requires"=>[], # "ignored_parameters"=> # ["CCSDSVER", # "CCSDSTYPE", # "CCSDSSHF", # "CCSDSAPID", # "CCSDSSEQFLAGS", # "CCSDSSEQCNT", # "CCSDSLENGTH", # "PKTID"], # "ignored_items"=> # ["CCSDSVER", # "CCSDSTYPE", # "CCSDSSHF", # "CCSDSAPID", # "CCSDSSEQFLAGS", # "CCSDSSEQCNT", # "CCSDSLENGTH", # "RECEIVED_COUNT", # "RECEIVED_TIMESECONDS", # "RECEIVED_TIMEFORMATTED"], # "limits_groups"=>[], # "cmd_tlm_files"=> # [".../targets/INST/cmd_tlm/inst_cmds.txt", # ".../targets/INST/cmd_tlm/inst_tlm.txt"], # "cmd_unique_id_mode"=>false, # "tlm_unique_id_mode"=>false, # "id"=>nil, # "updated_at"=>1613077058266815900, # "plugin"=>nil}

searchDocs.title: get_target_interfaces​
searchDocs.content: Returns the interfaces for all targets. The return value is an array of arrays where each subarray contains the target name, and a String of all the interface names.  Ruby ExamplePython Example target_ints = get_target_interfaces() target_ints.each do |target_name, interfaces| puts "Target: #{target_name}, Interfaces: #{interfaces}" end

searchDocs.title: Interfaces​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to manipulate COSMOS interfaces.

searchDocs.title: get_interface​
searchDocs.content: Returns an interface status including the as built interface and its current status (cmd/tlm counters, etc).  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_interface("<Interface Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface.  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface = get_interface("INST_INT") puts interface # {"name"=>"INST_INT", # "config_params"=>["interface.rb"], # "target_names"=>["INST"], # "connect_on_startup"=>true, # "auto_reconnect"=>true, # "reconnect_delay"=>5.0, # "disable_disconnect"=>false, # "options"=>[], # "protocols"=>[], # "log"=>true, # "log_raw"=>false, # "plugin"=>nil, # "updated_at"=>1613076213535979900, # "state"=>"CONNECTED", # "clients"=>0, # "txsize"=>0, # "rxsize"=>0, # "txbytes"=>0, # "rxbytes"=>0, # "txcnt"=>0, # "rxcnt"=>0}

searchDocs.title: get_interface_names​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of the interfaces in the system in an array.  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_names = get_interface_names() #=> ['INST_INT', 'INST2_INT', 'EXAMPLE_INT', 'TEMPLATED_INT']

searchDocs.title: connect_interface​
searchDocs.content: Connects to targets associated with a COSMOS interface.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax connect_interface("<Interface Name>", <Interface Parameters (optional)>)   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface. Interface Parameters	Parameters used to initialize the interface. If none are given then the interface will use the parameters that were given in the server configuration file.  Ruby ExamplePython Example connect_interface("INT1") connect_interface("INT1", hostname, port)

searchDocs.title: disconnect_interface​
searchDocs.content: Disconnects from targets associated with a COSMOS interface.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax disconnect_interface("<Interface Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disconnect_interface("INT1")

searchDocs.title: start_raw_logging_interface​
searchDocs.content: Starts logging of raw data on one or all interfaces. This is for debugging purposes only.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax start_raw_logging_interface("<Interface Name (optional)>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the Interface to command to start raw data logging. Defaults to 'ALL' which causes all interfaces that support raw data logging to start logging raw data.  Ruby ExamplePython Example start_raw_logging_interface("int1")

searchDocs.title: stop_raw_logging_interface​
searchDocs.content: Stops logging of raw data on one or all interfaces. This is for debugging purposes only.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax stop_raw_logging_interface("<Interface Name (optional)>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the Interface to command to stop raw data logging. Defaults to 'ALL' which causes all interfaces that support raw data logging to stop logging raw data.  Ruby ExamplePython Example stop_raw_logging_interface("int1")

searchDocs.title: get_all_interface_info​
searchDocs.content: Returns information about all interfaces. The return value is an array of arrays where each subarray contains the interface name, connection state, number of connected clients, transmit queue size, receive queue size, bytes transmitted, bytes received, command count, and telemetry count.  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_info = get_all_interface_info() interface_info.each do |interface_name, connection_state, num_clients, tx_q_size, rx_q_size, tx_bytes, rx_bytes, cmd_count, tlm_count| puts "Interface: #{interface_name}, Connection state: #{connection_state}, Num connected clients: #{num_clients}" puts "Transmit queue size: #{tx_q_size}, Receive queue size: #{rx_q_size}, Bytes transmitted: #{tx_bytes}, Bytes received: #{rx_bytes}" puts "Cmd count: #{cmd_count}, Tlm count: #{tlm_count}" end

searchDocs.title: map_target_to_interface​
searchDocs.content: Map a target to an interface allowing target commands and telemetry to be processed by that interface. Note this will cause the interface to respawn.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax map_target_to_interface("<Target Name>", "<Interface Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false, unmap_old: true)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target Interface Name	Name of the interface cmd_only	Named parameter whether to map target commands only to the interface (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to map target telemetry only to the interface (default: false) unmap_old	Named parameter whether remove the target from all existing interfaces (default: true)  Ruby ExamplePython Example map_target_to_interface("INST", "INST_INT", unmap_old: false) map_target_to_interface("INST", "INST_INT", cmd_only: true) map_target_to_interface("INST", "INST_INT", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: unmap_target_from_interface​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Unmap a target from an interface. This removes all knowledge of the target and will cause the interface to respawn.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax unmap_target_from_interface("<Target Name>", "<Interface Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target Interface Name	Name of the interface cmd_only	Named parameter whether to unmap target commands only to the interface (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to unmap target telemetry only to the interface (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example unmap_target_from_interface("INST", "INST_INT") unmap_target_from_interface("INST", "INST_INT", cmd_only: true) unmap_target_from_interface("INST", "INST_INT", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: interface_cmd​
searchDocs.content: Send a command directly to an interface. This has no effect in the standard COSMOS interfaces but can be implemented by a custom interface to change behavior.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax interface_cmd("<Interface Name>", "<Command Name>", "<Command Parameters>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface Command Name	Name of the command to send Command Parameters	Any parameters to send with the command  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_cmd("INST", "DISABLE_CRC")

searchDocs.title: interface_protocol_cmd​
searchDocs.content: Send a command directly to an interface protocol. This has no effect in the standard COSMOS protocols but can be implemented by a custom protocol to change behavior.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax interface_protocol_cmd("<Interface Name>", "<Command Name>", "<Command Parameters>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface Command Name	Name of the command to send Command Parameters	Any parameters to send with the command read_write	Whether command gets send to read or write protocols. Must be one of READ, WRITE, or READ_WRITE (Ruby symbols, Python strings). The default is READ_WRITE. index	Which protocol in the stack the command should apply to. The default is -1 which applies the command to all.  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_protocol_cmd("INST", "DISABLE_CRC", read_write: :READ_WRITE, index: -1)

searchDocs.title: interface_target_enable​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Enable commanding and telemetry processing for a given target in an interface.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax interface_target_enable("<Interface Name>", "<Target Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface Target Name	Name of the target cmd_only	Named parameter whether to enable target commands only to the interface (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to enable target telemetry only to the interface (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_target_enable("INST_INT", "INST") interface_target_enable("INST_INT", "INST", cmd_only: true) interface_target_enable("INST_INT", "INST", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: interface_target_disable​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Disable commanding and telemetry processing for a given target in an interface.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax interface_target_disable("<Interface Name>", "<Target Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface Target Name	Name of the target cmd_only	Named parameter whether to disable target commands only to the interface (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to disable target telemetry only to the interface (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_target_disable("INST_INT", "INST") interface_target_disable("INST_INT", "INST", cmd_only: true) interface_target_disable("INST_INT", "INST", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: interface_details​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Get details on the interface and its protocols.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax interface_details("<Interface Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionInterface Name	Name of the interface  Ruby ExamplePython Example interface_details("INST_INT")

searchDocs.title: Routers​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to manipulate COSMOS routers.

searchDocs.title: connect_router​
searchDocs.content: Connects a COSMOS router.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax connect_router("<Router Name>", <Router Parameters (optional)>)   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router. Router Parameters	Parameters used to initialize the router. If none are given then the router will use the parameters that were given in the server configuration file.  Ruby ExamplePython Example connect_router("INST_ROUTER") connect_router("INST_ROUTER", 7779, 7779, nil, 10.0, 'PREIDENTIFIED')

searchDocs.title: disconnect_router​
searchDocs.content: Disconnects a COSMOS router.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax disconnect_router("<Router Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disconnect_router("INT1_ROUTER")

searchDocs.title: get_router_names​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of the routers in the system in an array.  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_names = get_router_names() #=> ['ROUTER_INT']

searchDocs.title: get_router​
searchDocs.content: Returns a router status including the as built router and its current status (cmd/tlm counters, etc).  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_router("<Router Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router.  Ruby ExamplePython Example router = get_router("ROUTER_INT") puts router #{"name"=>"ROUTER_INT", # "config_params"=>["router.rb"], # "target_names"=>["INST"], # "connect_on_startup"=>true, # "auto_reconnect"=>true, # "reconnect_delay"=>5.0, # "disable_disconnect"=>false, # "options"=>[], # "protocols"=>[], # "log"=>true, # "log_raw"=>false, # "plugin"=>nil, # "updated_at"=>1613076213535979900, # "state"=>"CONNECTED", # "clients"=>0, # "txsize"=>0, # "rxsize"=>0, # "txbytes"=>0, # "rxbytes"=>0, # "txcnt"=>0, # "rxcnt"=>0}

searchDocs.title: get_all_router_info​
searchDocs.content: Returns information about all routers. The return value is an array of arrays where each subarray contains the router name, connection state, number of connected clients, transmit queue size, receive queue size, bytes transmitted, bytes received, packets received, and packets sent.  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_info = get_all_router_info() router_info.each do |router_name, connection_state, num_clients, tx_q_size, rx_q_size, tx_bytes, rx_bytes, pkts_rcvd, pkts_sent| puts "Router: #{router_name}, Connection state: #{connection_state}, Num connected clients: #{num_clients}" puts "Transmit queue size: #{tx_q_size}, Receive queue size: #{rx_q_size}, Bytes transmitted: #{tx_bytes}, Bytes received: #{rx_bytes}" puts "Packets received: #{pkts_rcvd}, Packets sent: #{pkts_sent}" end

searchDocs.title: map_target_to_router​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Map a target to an router allowing target commands and telemetry to be processed by that router. Note: this will cause the router to respawn.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax map_target_to_router("<Target Name>", "<Router Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false, unmap_old: true)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target Router Name	Name of the router cmd_only	Named parameter whether to map target commands only to the router (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to map target telemetry only to the router (default: false) unmap_old	Named parameter whether remove the target from all existing routers (default: true)  Ruby ExamplePython Example map_target_to_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER", unmap_old: false) map_target_to_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER", cmd_only: true) map_target_to_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: unmap_target_from_router​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Unmap a target from an router. This removes all knowledge of the target and will cause the router to respawn.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax unmap_target_from_router("<Target Name>", "<Router Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Name of the target Router Name	Name of the router cmd_only	Named parameter whether to unmap target commands only to the router (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to unmap target telemetry only to the router (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example unmap_target_from_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER") unmap_target_from_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER", cmd_only: true) unmap_target_from_router("INST", "INST_ROUTER", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: start_raw_logging_router​
searchDocs.content: Starts logging of raw data on one or all routers. This is for debugging purposes only.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax start_raw_logging_router("<Router Name (optional)>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the Router to command to start raw data logging. Defaults to 'ALL' which causes all routers that support raw data logging to start logging raw data.  Ruby ExamplePython Example start_raw_logging_router("router1")

searchDocs.title: stop_raw_logging_router​
searchDocs.content: Stops logging of raw data on one or all routers. This is for debugging purposes only.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax stop_raw_logging_router("<Router Name (optional)>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the Router to command to stop raw data logging. Defaults to 'ALL' which causes all routers that support raw data logging to stop logging raw data.  Ruby ExamplePython Example stop_raw_logging_router("router1")

searchDocs.title: router_cmd​
searchDocs.content: Send a command directly to a router. This has no effect in the standard COSMOS routers but can be implemented by a custom router to change behavior.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax router_cmd("<Router Name>", "<Command Name>", "<Command Parameters>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router Command Name	Name of the command to send Command Parameters	Any parameters to send with the command  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_cmd("INST", "DISABLE_CRC")

searchDocs.title: router_protocol_cmd​
searchDocs.content: Send a command directly to an router protocol. This has no effect in the standard COSMOS protocols but can be implemented by a custom protocol to change behavior.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax router_protocol_cmd("<Router Name>", "<Command Name>", "<Command Parameters>", read_write, index)   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router Command Name	Name of the command to send Command Parameters	Any parameters to send with the command read_write	Whether command gets send to read or write protocols. Must be one of READ, WRITE, or READ_WRITE (Ruby symbols, Python strings). The default is READ_WRITE. index	Which protocol in the stack the command should apply to. The default is -1 which applies the command to all.  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_protocol_cmd("INST", "DISABLE_CRC", read_write: :READ_WRITE, index: -1)

searchDocs.title: router_target_enable​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Enable commanding and telemetry processing for a given target in a router.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax router_target_enable("<Router Name>", "<Target Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router Target Name	Name of the target cmd_only	Named parameter whether to enable target commands only to the router (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to enable target telemetry only to the router (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_target_enable("INST_ROUTER", "INST") router_target_enable("INST_ROUTER", "INST", cmd_only: true) router_target_enable("INST_ROUTER", "INST", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: router_target_disable​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Disable commanding and telemetry processing for a given target in a router.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax router_target_disable("<Router Name>", "<Target Name>", cmd_only: false, tlm_only: false)   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router Target Name	Name of the target cmd_only	Named parameter whether to disable target commands only to the router (default: false) tlm_only	Named parameter whether to disable target telemetry only to the router (default: false)  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_target_disable("INST_ROUTER", "INST") router_target_disable("INST_ROUTER", "INST", cmd_only: true) router_target_disable("INST_ROUTER", "INST", tlm_only: true)

searchDocs.title: router_details​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.9.0  Get details on the router and its protocols.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax router_details("<Router Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionRouter Name	Name of the router  Ruby ExamplePython Example router_details("INST_ROUTER")

searchDocs.title: Tables​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to script Table Manager.

searchDocs.title: table_create_binary​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.1.0  Creates a table binary based on a table definition file. You can achieve the same result in the Table Manager GUI with File->New File. Returns the path to the binary file created.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax table_create_binary(<Table Definition File>)   Parameter	DescriptionTable Definition File	Path to the table definition file, e.g. INST/tables/config/ConfigTables_def.txt  Ruby ExamplePython Example # Full example of using table_create_binary and then editing the binary require 'openc3/tools/table_manager/table_config' # This returns a hash: {"filename"=>"INST/tables/bin/MCConfigurationTable.bin"} table = table_create_binary("INST/tables/config/MCConfigurationTable_def.txt") file = get_target_file(table['filename']) table_binary = file.read() # Get the definition file so we can process the binary def_file = get_target_file("INST/tables/config/MCConfigurationTable_def.txt") # Access the internal TableConfig to process the definition config = OpenC3::TableConfig.process_file(def_file.path()) # Grab the table by the definition name, e.g. TABLE "MC_Configuration" table = config.table('MC_CONFIGURATION') # Now you can read or write individual items in the table table.write("MEMORY_SCRUBBING", "DISABLE") # Finally write the table.buffer (the binary) back to storage put_target_file("INST/tables/bin/MCConfigurationTable_NoScrub.bin", table.buffer)

searchDocs.title: table_create_report​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.1.0  Creates a table binary based on a table definition file. You can achieve the same result in the Table Manager GUI with File->New File. Returns the path to the binary file created.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax table_create_report(<Table Binary Filename>, <Table Definition File>, <Table Name (optional)>)   filename, definition, table_name  Parameter	DescriptionTable Binary File	Path to the table binary file, e.g. INST/tables/bin/ConfigTables.bin Table Definition File	Path to the table definition file, e.g. INST/tables/config/ConfigTables_def.txt Table Name	Name of the table to create the report. This only applies if the Table Binary and Table Definition consist of multiple tables. By default the report consists of all tables and is named after the binary file. If the table name is given, the report is just the specified table and is named after the table.  Ruby ExamplePython Example table = table_create_report("INST/tables/bin/ConfigTables.bin", "INST/tables/config/ConfigTables_def.txt") #=> # {"filename"=>"INST/tables/bin/ConfigTables.csv", "contents"=>"MC_CONFIGURATION\nLabel, ... table = table_create_report("INST/tables/bin/ConfigTables.bin", "INST/tables/config/ConfigTables_def.txt", table_name: "MC_CONFIGURATION") #=> # {"filename"=>"INST/tables/bin/McConfiguration.csv", "contents"=>"MC_CONFIGURATION\nLabel, ...

searchDocs.title: Stashing Data​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to store data into COSMOS and retrieve it. The storage is implemented as a key / value storage (Ruby hash or Python dict). This can be used in scripts to store information that applies across multiple scripts or multiple runs of a single script. As a best practice, use stashes to store transient data, since duplicate keys will be overwritten by the most recent write.

searchDocs.title: stash_set​
searchDocs.content: Sets a stash item.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax stash_set("<Stash Key>", <Stash Value>)   Parameter	DescriptionStash Key	Name of the stash key to set Stash Value	Value to set  Ruby ExamplePython Example stash_set('run_count', 5) stash_set('setpoint', 23.4)

searchDocs.title: stash_get​
searchDocs.content: Returns the specified stash item.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax stash_get("<Stash Key>")   Parameter	DescriptionStash Key	Name of the stash key to return  Ruby ExamplePython Example stash_get('run_count') #=> 5

searchDocs.title: stash_all​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the stash items as a Ruby hash or Python dict.  Ruby ExamplePython Example stash_all() #=> ['run_count' => 5, 'setpoint' => 23.4]

searchDocs.title: stash_keys​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the stash keys.  Ruby ExamplePython Example stash_keys() #=> ['run_count', 'setpoint']

searchDocs.title: stash_delete​
searchDocs.content: Deletes a stash item. Note this actions is permanent!  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax stash_delete("<Stash Key>")   Parameter	DescriptionStash Key	Name of the stash key to delete  Ruby ExamplePython Example stash_delete("run_count")

searchDocs.title: Telemetry Screens​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to open, close or create unique telemetry screens from within a test procedure.

searchDocs.title: display_screen​
searchDocs.content: Opens a telemetry screen at the specified position.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax display_screen("<Target Name>", "<Screen Name>", <X Position (optional)>, <Y Position (optional)>)   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Telemetry screen target name Screen Name	Screen name within the specified target X Position	X coordinate for the upper left hand corner of the screen Y Position	Y coordinate for the upper left hand corner of the screen  Ruby ExamplePython Example display_screen("INST", "ADCS", 100, 200)

searchDocs.title: clear_screen​
searchDocs.content: Closes an open telemetry screen.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax clear_screen("<Target Name>", "<Screen Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Telemetry screen target name. Note for local screens the target is 'LOCAL'. Screen Name	Screen name within the specified target  Ruby ExamplePython Example clear_screen("INST", "ADCS")

searchDocs.title: clear_all_screens​
searchDocs.content: Closes all open screens.  Ruby ExamplePython Example clear_all_screens()

searchDocs.title: delete_screen​
searchDocs.content: Deletes an existing Telemetry Viewer screen.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax delete_screen("<Target Name>", "<Screen Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Telemetry screen target name Screen Name	Screen name within the specified target  Ruby ExamplePython Example delete_screen("INST", "ADCS")

searchDocs.title: get_screen_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of available telemetry screens.  Ruby ExamplePython Example get_screen_list() #=> ['INST ADCS', 'INST COMMANDING', ...]

searchDocs.title: get_screen_definition​
searchDocs.content: Returns the text file contents of a telemetry screen definition.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_screen_definition("<Target Name>", "<Screen Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Telemetry screen target name Screen Name	Screen name within the specified target  Ruby ExamplePython Example screen_definition = get_screen_definition("INST", "HS")

searchDocs.title: create_screen​
searchDocs.content: Allows you to create a screen directly from a script. This screen is saved to Telemetry Viewer for future use in that application.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax create_screen("<Target Name>", "<Screen Name>" "<Definition>")   Parameter	DescriptionTarget Name	Telemetry screen target name Screen Name	Screen name within the specified target Definition	The entire screen definition as a String  Ruby ExamplePython Example screen_def = ' SCREEN AUTO AUTO 0.1 VERTICAL TITLE "New Screen" VERTICALBOX LABELVALUE INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1 END END ' # Here we pass in the screen definition as a string create_screen("INST", "LOCAL", screen_def)

searchDocs.title: local_screen​
searchDocs.content: Allows you to create a local screen directly from a script which is not permanently saved to the Telemetry Viewer screen list. This is useful for one off screens that help users interact with scripts.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax local_screen("<Screen Name>", "<Definition>", <X Position (optional)>, <Y Position (optional)>)   Parameter	DescriptionScreen Name	Screen name within the specified target Definition	The entire screen definition as a String X Position	X coordinate for the upper left hand corner of the screen Y Position	Y coordinate for the upper left hand corner of the screen  NOTE: It is possible to specify a X, Y location off the visible display. If you do so and try to re-create the screen it will not display (because it is already displayed). Try issuing a clear_all_screens() first to clear any screens off the visible display space.  Ruby ExamplePython Example screen_def = ' SCREEN AUTO AUTO 0.1 VERTICAL TITLE "Local Screen" VERTICALBOX LABELVALUE INST HEALTH_STATUS TEMP1 END END ' # Here we pass in the screen definition as a string local_screen("TESTING", screen_def, 600, 75) wait 5 # The local screen 'target' is 'LOCAL' clear_screen("LOCAL", "TESTING")

searchDocs.title: Script Runner Scripts​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to control Script Runner scripts.

searchDocs.title: start​
searchDocs.content: Starts execution of another high level test procedure. Script Runner will load the file and immediately start executing it before jumping back to the calling procedure. Parameters are not directly given to high level test procedures, though they can use environment variables. If parameters are necessary, consider using a subroutine.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax start("<Procedure Filename>")   Parameter	DescriptionProcedure Filename	Name of the test procedure file. These files are normally in the procedures folder but may be anywhere in the Ruby search path. Additionally, absolute paths are supported.  Ruby ExamplePython Example start("test1.rb")

searchDocs.title: goto​
searchDocs.content: Since 6.4.0  Jumps to a specific line in either the current file or another file. Script context and local variables are retained if jumping in the same file. This method should not be used to create loops or in any other case where normal Python/Ruby functionality could perform the same function. Use of goto increases stack depth and excess use can lead to stack level too deep errors.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax goto(line_number) goto(filename, line_number)   Parameter	DescriptionLine Number or Procedure Filename	If this is the only argument given then it is the line number to goto in the same script. If two arguments are given, then it is the filename to goto Line Number	If the second argument, it is the line number in the filename given by the first argument to goto  Ruby ExamplePython Example goto(23) goto("TARGET/procedures/other_script.rb", 5) goto("TARGET/procedures/other_script.py", 12)

searchDocs.title: load_utility​
searchDocs.content: Reads in a script file that contains useful subroutines for use in your test procedure. When these subroutines run in ScriptRunner or TestRunner, their lines will be highlighted. If you want to import subroutines but do not want their lines to be highlighted in ScriptRunner or TestRunner, use the standard Ruby 'load' or 'require' statement or Python 'import' statement.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax load_utility("TARGET/lib/<Utility Filename>")   Parameter	DescriptionUtility Filename	Name of the script file containing subroutines including the .rb or .py extension. You need to include the full target name and path such as TARGET/lib/utility.rb  Ruby ExamplePython Example load_utility("TARGET/lib/mode_changes.rb") # Ruby load_utility("TARGET/lib/mode_changes.py") # Python

searchDocs.title: script_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the available files in COSMOS as an array / list. This includes configuration files at every directory level to ensure the user has access to every file. You can filter the list client side to just the 'lib' and or 'procedures' directories if you wish. Note: script names do NOT include '*' to indicate modified.  Ruby ExamplePython Example scripts = script_list() puts scripts.length #=> 139 puts scripts.select {|script| script.include?('/lib/') || script.include?('/procedures/')} #=> # [EXAMPLE/lib/example_interface.rb, INST/lib/example_limits_response.rb, ...]

searchDocs.title: script_create​
searchDocs.content: Creates a new script with the given contents.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_create("<Script Name>", "<Script Contents>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target Script Contents	Script contents as text  Ruby ExamplePython Example contents = 'puts "Hello from Ruby"' script_create("INST/procedures/new_script.rb", contents)

searchDocs.title: script_body​
searchDocs.content: Returns the script contents.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_body("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example script = script_body("INST/procedures/checks.rb") puts script #=> # Display all environment variables\nputs ENV.inspect ...

searchDocs.title: script_delete​
searchDocs.content: Deletes a script from COSMOS. Note, you can only really delete TEMP scripts and modified scripts. Scripts that are part of an installed COSMOS plugin remain as they were installed.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_delete("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example script_delete("INST/procedures/checks.rb")

searchDocs.title: script_run​
searchDocs.content: Runs a script in Script Runner. The script will run in the background and can be opened in Script Runner by selecting Script->Execution Status and then connecting to it.  Note: In Enterprise, initialize_offline_access must have been called at least once for the user who calls this method.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_run("<Script Name>", disconnect: false, environment: nil, suite_runner: nil)   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target disconnect	Whether to run the script in Disconnect mode environment	Hash / dict of key / value items to set as script environment variables. Note: Do not use PATH as it is reserved. suite_runner	Hash / dict of suite runner options  Ruby ExamplePython Example id = script_run("INST/procedures/checks.rb", environment: { USER: 'JASON'}) puts id id = script_run("INST/procedures/my_script_suite.rb", suite_runner: { suite: 'MySuite', group: 'ExampleGroup', script: 'script_2' }) puts id

searchDocs.title: script_lock​
searchDocs.content: Locks a script for editing. Subsequent users that open this script will get a warning that the script is currently locked.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_lock("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example script_lock("INST/procedures/checks.rb")

searchDocs.title: script_unlock​
searchDocs.content: Unlocks a script for editing. If the script was not previously locked this does nothing.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_unlock("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example script_unlock("INST/procedures/checks.rb")

searchDocs.title: script_syntax_check​
searchDocs.content: Performs a Ruby or Python syntax check on the given script.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_syntax_check("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example result = script_syntax_check("INST/procedures/checks.rb") puts result #=> {"title"=>"Syntax Check Successful", "description"=>"[\"Syntax OK\\n\"]", "success"=>true}

searchDocs.title: script_instrumented​
searchDocs.content: Returns the instrumented script which allows COSMOS Script Runner to monitor the execution and provide line by line visualization. This is primarily a low level debugging method used by COSMOS developers.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_instrumented("<Script Name>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Name	Full path name of the script starting with the target  Ruby ExamplePython Example script = script_instrumented("INST/procedures/checks.rb") puts script #=> private; __return_val = nil; begin; RunningScript.instance.script_binding = binding(); ...

searchDocs.title: script_delete_all_breakpoints​
searchDocs.content: Delete all breakpoints associated with all scripts.  Ruby ExamplePython Example script_delete_all_breakpoints()

searchDocs.title: step_mode​
searchDocs.content: Places ScriptRunner into step mode where Go must be hit to proceed to the next line.  Ruby ExamplePython Example step_mode()

searchDocs.title: run_mode​
searchDocs.content: Places ScriptRunner into run mode where the next line is run automatically.  Ruby ExamplePython Example run_mode()

searchDocs.title: disconnect_script​
searchDocs.content: Puts scripting into disconnect mode. In disconnect mode, commands are not sent to targets, checks are all successful, and waits expire instantly. Requests for telemetry (tlm()) typically return 0. Disconnect mode is useful for dry-running scripts without having connected targets.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disconnect_script()

searchDocs.title: running_script_list​
searchDocs.content: List the currently running scripts. Note, this will also include the script which is calling this method. Thus the list will never be empty but will always contain at least 1 item. Returns an array of hashes / list of dicts (see script_get for hash / dict contents).  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_list(limit: <limit>, offset: <offset>)   Parameter	Descriptionlimit	Max number to return (default 10) offset	Offset into list to return (default 0)  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_list(limit: 20, offset: 10) #=> [{"name"=>"293", "state"=>"waiting", "shard"=>0, "filename"=>"INST/procedures/collect.rb", "current_filename"=>"INST/procedures/collect.rb", "line_no"=>4, "start_line_no"=>1, "end_line_no"=>nil, "username"=>"anonymous", "user_full_name"=>"Anonymous", "start_time"=>"2025-05-03T04:26:50Z", "end_time"=>nil, "disconnect"=>false, "environment"=>"{}", "suite_runner"=>nil, "errors"=>nil, "pid"=>414, "log"=>nil, "report"=>nil, "updated_at"=>1746246411160414638, "scope"=>"DEFAULT"}]

searchDocs.title: script_get​
searchDocs.content: Get information on the script with the specified ID. The information returned is the script name (id), state (spawning, init, running, paused, waiting, error, breakpoint, crashed, stopped, completed, completed_errors, killed), shard, filename, current_filename, line_no, start_line_no, end_line_no, username, user_full_name, start_time, end_time, disconnect state, environment variables, suite_runner configuration, errors, pid, log file, report file, update time, and scope.  Possible script states:  spawning - Not running yetinit - Initializingrunning - Runningpaused - Pausedwaiting - Waiting (either for a wait or a prompt)error - Paused with an errorbreakpoint - Paused with a breakpoingcrashed - Complete after crashing with an exceptionstopped - Prematurely stoppedcompleted - Completed successfullycompleted_errors - Completed but errors occurred while runningkilled - Forcefully killed  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax script_get("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example script_get(15) #=> {"name"=>"293", "state"=>"waiting", "shard"=>0, "filename"=>"INST/procedures/collect.rb", "current_filename"=>"INST/procedures/collect.rb", "line_no"=>4, "start_line_no"=>1, "end_line_no"=>nil, "username"=>"anonymous", "user_full_name"=>"Anonymous", "start_time"=>"2025-05-03T04:26:50Z", "end_time"=>nil, "disconnect"=>false, "environment"=>"{}", "suite_runner"=>nil, "errors"=>nil, "pid"=>414, "log"=>nil, "report"=>nil, "updated_at"=>1746246411160414638, "scope"=>"DEFAULT"}

searchDocs.title: running_script_stop​
searchDocs.content: Stop the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Stop button in the Script Runner GUI.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_stop("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_stop(15)

searchDocs.title: running_script_pause​
searchDocs.content: Pause the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Pause button in the Script Runner GUI.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_pause("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_pause(15)

searchDocs.title: running_script_retry​
searchDocs.content: Retry the current line of the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Retry button in the Script Runner GUI.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_retry("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_retry(15)

searchDocs.title: running_script_go​
searchDocs.content: Unpause the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Go button in the Script Runner GUI.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_go("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_go(15)

searchDocs.title: running_script_execute_while_paused​
searchDocs.content: Perform a goto or execute selection on a running script.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_execute_while_paused("<Script Id>", "<Script File Path>", <Start Line Number>, <End Line Number>)   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run Script File Path	Path to to the script to run ie. "INST/procedures/collect.rb" Start Line Number	Line Number to Start At End Line Number	Optional - Line Number to End At. If given then execute selection, else goto.  Ruby ExamplePython Example # Execute Selection running_script_execute_while_paused("23", "INST/procedures/myprocedure.rb", 2, 4) # Goto running_script_execute_while_paused("33", "INST2/procedures/collect.py", 7)

searchDocs.title: running_script_step​
searchDocs.content: Step the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Step button in the Script Runner GUI's Debug window.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_step("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_step(15)

searchDocs.title: running_script_delete​
searchDocs.content: Force quit the running script with the specified ID. This is equivalent to clicking the Delete button under the Running Scripts in the Script Runner GUI's Script -> Execution Status pane. Note, the 'stop' signal is first sent to the specified script and then the script is forcibly removed. Normally you should use the running_script_stop method.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax running_script_delete("<Script Id>")   Parameter	DescriptionScript Id	Script ID returned by script_run  Ruby ExamplePython Example running_script_delete(15)

searchDocs.title: completed_script_list​
searchDocs.content: List the completed scripts. Returns an array of hashes / list of dicts containing the id, username, script name, script log, and start time.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax completed_script_list(limit: <limit>, offset: <offset>)   Parameter	Descriptionlimit	Max number to return (default 10) offset	Offset into list to return (default 0)  Ruby ExamplePython Example completed_script_list(limit: 10, offset: 0) #=> [{"id"=>"15", "user"=>"Anonymous", "name"=>"__TEMP__/2025_01_15_17_07_51_568_temp.rb", "log"=>"DEFAULT/tool_logs/sr/20250116/2025_01_16_00_28_43_sr_2025_01_15_17_07_51_568_temp.txt", "start"=>"2025-01-16 00:28:43 +0000"}, ...]

searchDocs.title: Script Runner Settings​
searchDocs.content: These methods allow the user to control various Script Runner settings.

searchDocs.title: set_line_delay​
searchDocs.content: This method sets the line delay in script runner.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_line_delay(<Delay>)   Parameter	DescriptionDelay	The amount of time script runner will wait between lines when executing a script, in seconds. Should be ≥ 0.0  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_line_delay(0.0)

searchDocs.title: get_line_delay​
searchDocs.content: The method gets the line delay that script runner is currently using.  Ruby ExamplePython Example curr_line_delay = get_line_delay()

searchDocs.title: set_max_output​
searchDocs.content: This method sets the maximum number of characters to display in Script Runner output before truncating. Default is 50,000 characters.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_max_output(<Characters>)   Parameter	DescriptionCharacters	Number of characters to output before truncating  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_max_output(100)

searchDocs.title: get_max_output​
searchDocs.content: The method gets the maximum number of characters to display in Script Runner output before truncating. Default is 50,000 characters.  Ruby ExamplePython Example puts get_max_output() #=> 50000

searchDocs.title: disable_instrumentation​
searchDocs.content: Disables instrumentation for a block of code (line highlighting and exception catching). This is especially useful for speeding up loops that are very slow if lines are instrumented. Consider breaking code like this into a separate file and using either require/load to read the file for the same effect while still allowing errors to be caught by your script.  Use with Caution Disabling instrumentation will cause any error that occurs while disabled to cause your script to completely stop.  Ruby ExamplePython Example disable_instrumentation do 1000.times do # Don't want this to have to highlight 1000 times end end

searchDocs.title: Script Runner Suites​
searchDocs.content: Creating Script Runner suites utilizes APIs to add groups to the defined suites. For more information please see running script suites.

searchDocs.title: add_group, add_group_setup, add_group_teardown, add_script​
searchDocs.content: Adds a group's methods to the suite. The add_group method adds all the group methods including setup, teardown, and all the methods starting with 'script_' or 'test_'. The add_group_setup method adds just the setup method defined in the group class. The add_group_teardown method adds just the teardown method defined in the group class. The add_script method adds an individual method to the suite. NOTE: add_script can add any method including those not named with 'script_' or 'test_'.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax add_group(<Group Class>) add_group_setup(<Group Class>) add_group_teardown(<Group Class>) add_script(<Group Class>, <Method>)   Parameter	DescriptionGroup Class	Name of the previously defined class which inherits from the OpenC3 Group class. The Ruby API passes a String with the name of the group. The Python API passes the Group class directly. Method	Name of the method in the OpenC3 Group class. The Ruby API passes a String with the name of the method. The Python API passes the Group class directly.  Ruby ExamplePython Example load 'openc3/script/suite.rb' class ExampleGroup < OpenC3::Group def script_1 # Insert test code here ... end end class WrapperGroup < OpenC3::Group def setup # Insert test code here ... end def my_method # Insert test code here ... end def teardown # Insert test code here ... end end class MySuite < OpenC3::Suite def initialize super() add_group('ExampleGroup') add_group_setup('WrapperGroup') add_script('WrapperGroup', 'my_method') add_group_teardown('WrapperGroup') end end

searchDocs.title: Timelines​
searchDocs.content: The Timelines API allows you to manipulate Calendar timelines. Calendar is a COSMOS Enterprise tool.

searchDocs.title: list_timelines​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the timelines in an array of hashes / list of dicts.  Ruby ExamplePython Example timelines = list_timelines() #=> # [{"name"=>"Mine", "color"=>"#e67643", "execute"=>true, "shard"=>0, "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "updated_at"=>1737124024123643504}]

searchDocs.title: create_timeline​
searchDocs.content: Create a new timeline in Calendar which can hold activities.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax create_timeline(name, color: nil)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline color	Color of the timeline. Must be given as a hex value, e.g. #FF0000. Default is a random color.  Ruby ExamplePython Example tl = create_timeline("Mine") #=> # {"name"=>"Mine", "color"=>"#e67643", "execute"=>true, "shard"=>0, "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "updated_at"=>1737124024123643504}

searchDocs.title: get_timeline​
searchDocs.content: Get information about an existing timeline.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_timeline(name)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline  Ruby ExamplePython Example tl = get_timeline("Mine") #=> # {"name"=>"Mine", "color"=>"#e67643", "execute"=>true, "shard"=>0, "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "updated_at"=>1737124024123643504}

searchDocs.title: set_timeline_color​
searchDocs.content: Set the displayed color for an existing timeline.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_timeline_color(name, color)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline color	Color of the timeline. Must be given as a hex value, e.g. #FF0000.  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_timeline_color("Mine", "#4287f5")

searchDocs.title: delete_timeline​
searchDocs.content: Delete an existing timeline. Timelines with activities can only be deleted by passing force = true.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax delete_timeline(name, force: false)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline force	Whether to delete the timeline if it has activities. Default is false.  Ruby ExamplePython Example delete_timeline("Mine", force: true)

searchDocs.title: create_timeline_activity​
searchDocs.content: Create an activity on an existing timeline. Activities can be one of COMMAND, SCRIPT, or RESERVE. Activities have a start and stop time and commands and scripts take data on the command or script to execute.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax create_timeline_activity(name, kind:, start:, stop:, data: {})   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline kind	Type of the activity. One of COMMAND, SCRIPT, or RESERVE. start	Start time of the activity. Time / datetime instance. stop	Stop time of the activity. Time / datetime instance. data	Hash / dict of data for COMMAND or SCRIPT type. Default is empty hash / dict.  Ruby ExamplePython Example now = Time.now() start = now + 3600 stop = start + 3600 act = create_timeline_activity("RubyTL", kind: "RESERVE", start: start, stop: stop) #=> # { "name"=>"RubyTL", "updated_at"=>1737128705034982375, "start"=>1737132303, "stop"=>1737135903, # "kind"=>"reserve", "data"=>{"username"=>"operator"}, # "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "fulfillment"=>false, "uuid"=>"5f373846-eb6c-43cd-97bd-cca19a8ffb04", # "events"=>[{"time"=>1737128705, "event"=>"created"}], "recurring"=>{}} act = create_timeline_activity("RubyTL", kind: "COMMAND", start: start, stop: stop, data: {command: "INST COLLECT with TYPE NORMAL, DURATION 5, TEMP 10"}) #=> # { "name"=>"RubyTL", "updated_at"=>1737128761316084471, "start"=>1737132303, "stop"=>1737135903, # "kind"=>"command", "data"=>{"command"=>"INST COLLECT with TYPE NORMAL, DURATION 5, TEMP 10", "username"=>"operator"}, # "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "fulfillment"=>false, "uuid"=>"cdb661b4-a65b-44e7-95e2-5e1dba80c782", # "events"=>[{"time"=>1737128761, "event"=>"created"}], "recurring"=>{}} act = create_timeline_activity("RubyTL", kind: "SCRIPT", start: start, stop: stop, data: {environment: [{key: "USER", value: "JASON"}], script: "INST/procedures/checks.rb"}) #=> # { "name"=>"RubyTL", "updated_at"=>1737128791047885970, "start"=>1737135903, "stop"=>1737139503, # "kind"=>"script", "data"=>{"environment"=>[{"key"=>"USER", "value"=>"JASON"}], "script"=>"INST/procedures/checks.rb", "username"=>"operator"}, # "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "fulfillment"=>false, "uuid"=>"70426e3d-6313-4897-b159-6e5cd94ace1d", # "events"=>[{"time"=>1737128791, "event"=>"created"}], "recurring"=>{}}

searchDocs.title: get_timeline_activity​
searchDocs.content: Get an existing timeline activity.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_timeline_activity(name, start, uuid)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline start	Start time of the activity. Time / datetime instance. uuid	UUID of the activity  Ruby ExamplePython Example act = get_timeline_activity("RubyTL", 1737132303, "cdb661b4-a65b-44e7-95e2-5e1dba80c782") #=> # { "name"=>"RubyTL", "updated_at"=>1737128761316084471, "start"=>1737132303, "stop"=>1737135903, # "kind"=>"command", "data"=>{"command"=>"INST COLLECT with TYPE NORMAL, DURATION 5, TEMP 10", "username"=>"operator"}, # "scope"=>"DEFAULT", "fulfillment"=>false, "uuid"=>"cdb661b4-a65b-44e7-95e2-5e1dba80c782", # "events"=>[{"time"=>1737128761, "event"=>"created"}], "recurring"=>{}}

searchDocs.title: get_timeline_activities​
searchDocs.content: Get a range of timeline activities between start and stop time. If called without a start / stop time it defaults to 1 week before "now" up to 1 week from "now" (2 weeks total).  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_timeline_activities(name, start: nil, stop: nil, limit: nil)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline start	Start time of the activities. Time / datetime instance. Defaults to 7 days ago. stop	Stop time of the activities. Time / datetime instance. Defaults to 7 days from now. limit	Maximum number of activities to return. Default is 1 per minute of the time range.  Ruby ExamplePython Example acts = get_timeline_activities("RubyTL", start: Time.now() - 3600, stop: Time.now(), limit: 1000) #=> # [{ "name"=>"RubyTL", ... }, { "name"=>"RubyTL", ... }]

searchDocs.title: delete_timeline_activity​
searchDocs.content: Delete an existing timeline activity.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax delete_timeline_activity(name, start, uuid)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the timeline start	Start time of the activity. Time / datetime instance. uuid	UUID of the activity  Ruby ExamplePython Example delete_timeline_activity("RubyTL", 1737132303, "cdb661b4-a65b-44e7-95e2-5e1dba80c782")

searchDocs.title: Metadata​
searchDocs.content: Metadata allows you to mark the regular target / packet data logged in COSMOS with your own fields. This metadata can then be searched and used to filter data when using other COSMOS tools.

searchDocs.title: metadata_all​
searchDocs.content: Returns all the metadata that was previously set  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax metadata_all()   Parameter	Descriptionlimit	Amount of metadata items to return. Default is 100.  Ruby ExamplePython Example metadata_all(limit: 500)

searchDocs.title: metadata_get​
searchDocs.content: Returns metadata that was previously set  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax metadata_get(start)   Parameter	Descriptionstart	Named parameter, time at which to retrieve metadata as integer seconds from epoch  Ruby ExamplePython Example metadata_get(start: 500)

searchDocs.title: metadata_set​
searchDocs.content: Sets metadata which appears on the Calendar and can be retried by metadata_get.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax metadata_set(<Metadata>, start, color)   Parameter	DescriptionMetadata	Hash or dict of key value pairs to store as metadata. start	Named parameter, time at which to store metadata. Default is now. color	Named parameter, color to display metadata in the calendar. Default is #003784.  Ruby ExamplePython Example metadata_set({ 'key' => 'value' }) metadata_set({ 'key' => 'value' }, color: '#ff5252')

searchDocs.title: metadata_update​
searchDocs.content: Updates metadata that was previously set  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax metadata_update(<Metadata>, start, color)   Parameter	DescriptionMetadata	Hash or dict of key value pairs to update as metadata. start	Named parameter, time at which to update metadata. Default is latest metadata. color	Named parameter, color to display metadata in the calendar. Default is #003784.  Ruby ExamplePython Example metadata_update({ 'key' => 'value' })

searchDocs.title: metadata_input​
searchDocs.content: Prompts the user to set existing metadata values or create new a new one.  Ruby ExamplePython Example metadata_input()

searchDocs.title: Settings​
searchDocs.content: COSMOS has several settings typically accessed through the Admin Settings tab. These APIs allow programmatic access to those same settings.

searchDocs.title: list_settings​
searchDocs.content: Return all the current COSMOS setting name. These are the names that should be used in the other APIs.  Ruby ExamplePython Example puts list_settings() #=> ["pypi_url", "rubygems_url", "source_url", "version"]

searchDocs.title: get_all_settings​
searchDocs.content: Return all the current COSMOS settings along with their values.  Ruby ExamplePython Example settings = get_all_settings() #=> # { "version"=>{"name"=>"version", "data"=>"5.11.4-beta0", "updated_at"=>1698074299509456507}, # "pypi_url"=>{"name"=>"pypi_url", "data"=>"https://pypi.org/simple", "updated_at"=>1698026776574347007}, # "rubygems_url"=>{"name"=>"rubygems_url", "data"=>"https://rubygems.org", "updated_at"=>1698026776574105465}, # "source_url"=>{"name"=>"source_url", "data"=>"https://github.com/OpenC3/cosmos", "updated_at"=>1698026776573904132} }

searchDocs.title: get_setting, get_settings​
searchDocs.content: Return the data from the given COSMOS setting. Returns nil (Ruby) or None (Python) if the setting does not exist.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax get_setting(<Setting Name>) get_settings(<Setting Name1>, <Setting Name2>, ...)   Parameter	DescriptionSetting Name	Name of the setting to return  Ruby ExamplePython Example setting = get_setting('version') #=> "5.11.4-beta0" setting = get_settings('version', 'rubygems_url') #=> ["5.11.4-beta0", "https://rubygems.org"]

searchDocs.title: set_setting​
searchDocs.content: Sets the given setting value.  Admin Passwork Required This API is only accessible externally (not within Script Runner) and requires the admin password.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_setting(<Setting Name>, <Setting Value>)   Parameter	DescriptionSetting Name	Name of the setting to change Setting Value	Setting value to set  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_setting('rubygems_url', 'https://mygemserver')

searchDocs.title: Configuration​
searchDocs.content: Many COSMOS tools have the ability to load and save a configuration. These APIs allow you to programmatically load and save the configuration.

searchDocs.title: config_tool_names​
searchDocs.content: List all the configuration tool names which are used as the first parameter in the other APIs.  Ruby ExamplePython Example names = config_tool_names() #=> ["telemetry_grapher", "data_viewer"]

searchDocs.title: list_configs​
searchDocs.content: List all the saved configuration names under the given tool name.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax list_configs(<Tool Name>)   Parameter	DescriptionTool Name	Name of the tool to retrieve configuration names from  Ruby ExamplePython Example configs = list_configs('telemetry_grapher') #=> ['adcs', 'temps']

searchDocs.title: load_config​
searchDocs.content: Load a particular tool configuration.  Tool Configuration Tool configurations are not fully documented and subject to change between releases. Only modify values returned by load_config and do not change any keys.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax load_config(<Tool Name>, <Configuration Name>)   Parameter	DescriptionTool Name	Name of the tool Configuration Name	Name of the configuration  Ruby ExamplePython Example config = load_config('telemetry_grapher', 'adcs') #=> # [ { # "items": [ # { # "targetName": "INST", # "packetName": "ADCS", # "itemName": "CCSDSVER", # ...

searchDocs.title: save_config​
searchDocs.content: Save a particular tool configuration.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax save_config(<Tool Name>, <Configuration Name>, local_mode)   Parameter	DescriptionTool Name	Name of the tool Configuration Name	Name of the configuration local_mode	Whether to save the configuration in local mode  Ruby ExamplePython Example save_config('telemetry_grapher', 'adcs', config)

searchDocs.title: delete_config​
searchDocs.content: Delete a particular tool configuration.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax delete_config(<Tool Name>, <Configuration Name>, local_mode)   Parameter	DescriptionTool Name	Name of the tool Configuration Name	Name of the configuration local_mode	Whether to delete the configuration in local mode  Ruby ExamplePython Example delete_config('telemetry_grapher', 'adcs')

searchDocs.title: Offline Access​
searchDocs.content: An offline access token is required to execute scripts in COSMOS Enterprise. These methods support client side creation, testing, and setting of the offline_access_token.

searchDocs.title: initialize_offline_access​
searchDocs.content: Creates and sets the offline access token for the user. Note: calling this method is required before executing any api methods that require an offline access token like script_run (Enterprise Only). This method must be called OUTSIDE of ScriptRunner as it is needed in order to start a script in the first place.  In Enterprise, the OPENC3_API_USER and OPENC3_API_PASSWORD environment variables must be set for the initialize_offline_access to generate a token. This API user must also be a valid user with the respective permissions setup in Keycloak. These two variables are not in the .env by default, and should not be as they contain sensitive information.  Depending on your deployment environment, there are several ways Secrets can be managed. In a Kubernetes deployment, Secretes can be configured, or dynamically managed by a separate secrets management tool like HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager.  Ruby ExamplePython Example # First setup environment variables. See examples/external_script.rb initialize_offline_access() script_run("INST/procedures/collect.rb")

searchDocs.title: offline_access_needed​
searchDocs.content: Returns true if the user needs to generate an offline access token. Note this will only be true if the user is at least authorized to view scripts, otherwise it will always be false if script_view permission is not available for the user.  Ruby ExamplePython Example result = offline_access_needed() #=> true

searchDocs.title: set_offline_access​
searchDocs.content: Sets the offline access token in the backend. Note: You probably don't need to call this method directly, as it will be called by initialize_offline_access().  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax set_offline_access(offline_access_token)   Parameter	Descriptionoffline_access_token	Keycloak generated refresh token that contains the offline_access openid scope  Ruby ExamplePython Example set_offline_access(offline_access_token)

searchDocs.title: Autonomic​
searchDocs.content: Autonomic provides automated reactions to telemetry conditions through triggers and reactions. These methods allow you to manage autonomic groups, triggers, and reactions programmatically.

searchDocs.title: autonomic_group_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of all autonomic groups.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_group_list(scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionscope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example groups = autonomic_group_list()

searchDocs.title: autonomic_group_create​
searchDocs.content: Creates a new autonomic group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_group_create(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the group to create scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example group = autonomic_group_create("SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_group_show​
searchDocs.content: Shows details about a specific autonomic group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_group_show(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the group scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example group_info = autonomic_group_show("SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_group_destroy​
searchDocs.content: Destroys an autonomic group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_group_destroy(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the group to destroy scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_group_destroy("SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of triggers in a specific group.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_list(group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptiongroup	Group to list triggers from (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example triggers = autonomic_trigger_list(group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_create​
searchDocs.content: Creates a new trigger with the specified condition.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_create(left:, operator:, right:, group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionleft	Left side of the trigger condition operator	Comparison operator (e.g. >, <, ==, !=, >=, <=) right	Right side of the trigger condition group	Group to create the trigger in (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example # Create a test trigger left = { type: "item", target: "INST", packet: "HEALTH_STATUS", item: "TEMP1", valueType: "CONVERTED", } operator = ">" right = { type: "float", float: 0, } trigger = autonomic_trigger_create(left: left, operator: operator, right: right, group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_show​
searchDocs.content: Shows details about a specific trigger.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_show(name, group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the trigger group	Group the trigger belongs to (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example trigger_info = autonomic_trigger_show("TRIG1", group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_enable​
searchDocs.content: Enables a trigger.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_enable(name, group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the trigger to enable group	Group the trigger belongs to (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_trigger_enable("TRIG1", group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_disable​
searchDocs.content: Disables a trigger.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_disable(name, group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the trigger to disable group	Group the trigger belongs to (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_trigger_disable("TRIG1", group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_update​
searchDocs.content: Updates an existing trigger with new parameters.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_update(name, group: 'DEFAULT', left: left, operator: operator, right: right, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the trigger to update group	Group the trigger belongs to (default: 'DEFAULT') left	New left side of the trigger condition (optional) operator	New comparison operator (optional) right	New right side of the trigger condition (optional) scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example right = { type: "float", float: 100, } updated_trigger = autonomic_trigger_update( "TRIG1", group: "SAFETY", right: right )

searchDocs.title: autonomic_trigger_destroy​
searchDocs.content: Destroys a trigger.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_trigger_destroy(name, group: 'DEFAULT', scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the trigger to destroy group	Group the trigger belongs to (default: 'DEFAULT') scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_trigger_destroy("TRIG1", group: "SAFETY")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_list​
searchDocs.content: Returns a list of all reactions.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_list(scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionscope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example reactions = autonomic_reaction_list()

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_create​
searchDocs.content: Creates a new reaction that executes actions when triggers are activated.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_create(triggers:, actions:, trigger_level: 'EDGE', snooze: 0, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptiontriggers	List of trigger names that activate this reaction actions	List of actions to perform when triggered trigger_level	Trigger level: 'EDGE' or 'LEVEL' (default: 'EDGE') snooze	Snooze time in seconds between activations (default: 0) scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example triggers = [{ 'name' => 'TRIG1', 'group' => 'SAFETY', }] actions = [{ 'type' => 'command', 'value' => 'INST ABORT' }] reaction = autonomic_reaction_create( triggers: triggers, actions: actions, trigger_level: 'EDGE', snooze: 300 )

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_show​
searchDocs.content: Shows details about a specific reaction.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_show(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example reaction_info = autonomic_reaction_show("REACT1")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_enable​
searchDocs.content: Enables a reaction.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_enable(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction to enable scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_reaction_enable("REACT1")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_disable​
searchDocs.content: Disables a reaction.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_disable(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction to disable scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_reaction_disable("REACT1")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_execute​
searchDocs.content: Manually executes a reaction's actions.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_execute(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction to execute scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_reaction_execute("REACT1")

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_update​
searchDocs.content: Updates an existing reaction with new parameters.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_update(name, triggers: nil, actions: nil, trigger_level: nil, snooze: nil, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction to update triggers	New list of trigger names (optional) actions	New list of actions (optional) trigger_level	New trigger level (optional) snooze	New snooze time in seconds (optional) scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example updated_reaction = autonomic_reaction_update( "REACT1", snooze: 600 )

searchDocs.title: autonomic_reaction_destroy​
searchDocs.content: Destroys a reaction.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax autonomic_reaction_destroy(name, scope: $openc3_scope)   Parameter	Descriptionname	Name of the reaction to destroy scope	Scope to operate in (optional)  Ruby ExamplePython Example autonomic_reaction_destroy("REACT1")

searchDocs.title: Utilities​
searchDocs.content: Various utilities that can be used in scripts.

searchDocs.title: open_tab​
searchDocs.content: Opens a new tab in the browser.  Ruby SyntaxPython Syntax open_tab(url)   Parameter	Descriptionurl	URL to open in a new tab  Ruby ExamplePython Example open_tab("https://github.com/OpenC3/cosmos")
